<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The note graph issue is caused by double-encoded JSON data.

The script tag contains a quoted JSON string rather than raw JSON. This causes `JSON.parse` to fail when accessing `.nodes`. The `jsonify` filter in graph.html is likely wrapping an already-serialized value.

The root cause may differ from double-encoding. The partial returns a map, yet `jsonify` still produces a quoted string. SafeHTML or automatic escaping in the script tag could be the real culprit.

The fix is to pipe the `jsonify` filter through `safeJS` or `safeHTML` in the template. This prevents the JSON string from being escaped again inside the script tag.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The note graph issue is caused by double-encoded JSON.

Checking whether Hugo's `return` in partials converts the map to a string, causing `jsonify` to double-encode it.

Hugo 0.96+ supports returning values from partials. The graph-data.html partial contains only a return statement with no stray output.

HTML escaping was ruled out because the escaped quotes would appear as `&quot;`, not `\"`. The `\"` pattern confirms the data is a JSON string, meaning `jsonify` received a string rather than a map. The question remains why `$graph` is being treated as a string.

Testing whether Hugo coerces partial return values to strings when the partial contains only a return statement. Considering fixes such as using `safeHTML`, avoiding double `jsonify`, or changing the assignment syntax.

Several fixes were considered and rejected. Using `safeHTML` or skipping `jsonify` will not resolve the double encoding. Unmarshaling and re-jsonifying is too convoluted. Parsing the data twice in JavaScript is hacky. Outputting the dictionary directly is invalid in templates. A quick Hugo debug will verify how partial returns behave.

The partial returns a dictionary via `return`. The current hypothesis is that Hugo treats this return value as `template.HTML`, causing `jsonify` to quote it as a string instead of serializing the underlying map. A simpler fix may be to pass the dictionary directly to `jsonify` inline rather than through the partial variable, or to use `transform.Unmarshal`.

The partial contains only a return statement, ruling out extra rendered output. Hugo documentation confirms that partial return values are assigned directly to variables, so `$graph` should be the dictionary itself. This means `jsonify` is likely receiving a string rather than a map, pointing to a different cause for the double encoding.

Hugo 0.161.1 supports partial return values, so the version is not the problem. The node fields contain only plain strings, ruling out complex objects as the cause. The new suspicion is that the partial may be capturing empty output instead of the returned dictionary, or that a variable shadowing issue is interfering.

The JavaScript receives a string from the script element. After the first JSON.parse, the result is still a string, so accessing `.nodes` returns undefined. The code then displays the empty message and skips canvas drawing, which explains the broken graph.

The graph renders only five nodes instead of roughly seventy. Wikilinks in the raw markdown are not being detected. The matching logic correctly finds `[[Associative Linking]]` in atomic-notes.md, so the bug is likely in the partial's node assembly or the graph builder.

The partial `pages.html` may be filtering out most notes. Its `where` clauses exclude drafts and graph layouts, which could explain why only five pages reach the graph builder.</details></think>

The real culprit is likely Go's `html/template` engine. It automatically escapes quotes inside `<script>` tags, which turns the JSON string into a double-encoded string. That explains why the data arrives corrupted even though the template itself looks correct.

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

The note graph was failing because the graph data JSON was **double-encoded** in the page HTML.

Hugo’s template engine escapes content inside `<script>` tags. With `{{ $graph | jsonify }}`, the output was treated as a JavaScript string and wrapped in extra quotes, so the browser saw:

```json
"{\"nodes\":[...],\"edges\":[...]}"
```

instead of a real object. `JSON.parse()` then returned a **string**, not an object, so `data.nodes` was `undefined` and the graph script exited without drawing anything.

**Fix:** mark the JSON as safe for script context with `safeJS`:

```22:22:layouts/notes/graph.html
  <script type="application/json" id="notes-graph-data">{{ $graph | jsonify | safeJS }}</script>
```

After rebuilding, the data parses correctly as an object with **73 nodes** and hundreds of edges. Reload `/notes/graph/` and you should see the force-directed graph.

If you’re running `npm run dev`, restart or let Hugo rebuild so the template change is picked up.

---

This is the rendered-site equivalent of Obsidian’s local graph view — built from the same assets/data/wiki-index.json that powers the client-side resolver and backlinks panel.\n...\nTips: click a node to open the page · ⌘/Ctrl-click to open in a new tab · drag to reposition · scroll to zoom · hover to highlight a node's neighborhood · type in the search box to filter.\n...\n## How it’s built\n...\n| Piece | File |\n| --- | --- |\n| Build-time index (nodes + outgoing edges) | assets/data/wiki-index.json(Liquid template at assets/data/wiki-index.json) |\n| Renderer | &#96;assets/js/obsidian-graph.js&#96; |\n| Layout engine | cytoscape.js(loaded from CDN, only on this page) |\n...\nOutgoing edges come from the same&#96;[[…]]&#96; syntax the resolver handles — unresolved targets show up as dashed red nodes so you can find dangling links at a glance. Quartz already includes useful components such as search, dark mode, the explorer, graph view, backlinks, and a table of contents.\n...\nThe explorer uses the folder structure inside&#96;content&#96;, so folders naturally work like categories.\n...\nAfter the push, GitHub Actions builds the Quartz site and deploys it to GitHub Pages.\n...\nThe final site is served at:\n...\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nhttps://hanjoonbae.github.io/blog/\n...\nThis setup keeps the workflow small\n...\n- I write in Obsidian.\n- Markdown files stay in Git.\n- Quartz handles the static site.\n- GitHub Actions handles deployment.\n- GitHub Pages hosts the blog.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eBuilding a Knowledge Graph for My Blog\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://tom-dickson.com/blog/knowledge-graph/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eHow I built an interactive knowledge graph connecting blog posts through semantic similarity and shared tags. A deep dive into embeddings, NER, and D3 force-directed layouts.\n...\nI wanted to go beyond simple “related posts” recommendations. In my previous post\n...\n. But what if readers could explore\n...\nof connected ideas? A knowledge graph\n...\nprecisely this:\n...\n## Interactive Visualization\n...\nBelow is the live knowledge graph. Posts are colored by their k-means cluster assignment. You can see how embeddings group related content. Click any post to read it, or toggle “Tags” to see how topics connect through shared labels.\n...\nUnderstanding the colors: Posts are colored by k-means cluster assignment. The algorithm groups posts with similar embeddings into 10 clusters, each with a distinct color: blue, red, green, amber, violet, pink, cyan, orange, lime, and indigo. Posts about related topics cluster together automatically and share the same color. Tags appear as gray rounded rectangles to avoid competing visually with post nodes. Concepts (currently none) would appear as purple diamonds. The colors reveal semantic groupings discovered automatically from content, not manual categories.\n...\n## Visualization with D3\n...\nThe interactive visualization uses D3’s force-directed layout. Each node repels others (charge force) while edges pull connected nodes together (link force). The simulation settles into an equilibrium where clusters emerge naturally.\n...\n1. Collision detection: Posts receive a larger radius (20px) than tags (15px), preventing overlap\n2. Edge strength: Similarity edges use their weight as strength, with higher similarity pulling nodes closer\n3. Dynamic layers: Toggling tags adds or removes nodes and re-heats the simulation with smooth transitions\n4. Cluster coloring: Ten distinct colors map to the k-means cluster assignments from the generation step\n...\nThe component renders client-side only because D3 requires DOM access.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eThe Best Libraries and Methods to Render Large Force-Directed Graphs on the Web | by Stephen | Medium\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://weber-stephen.medium.com/the-best-libraries-and-methods-to-render-large-network-graphs-on-the-web-d122ece2f4dc\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eIn the world of data visualization, force-directed graphs are a powerful tool for representing complex relationships in a visually compelling way. These graphs are particularly useful for displaying network structures, such as social networks, molecular structures, and hierarchical data. However, rendering large force-directed graphs on the web poses significant challenges in terms of performance and interactivity. In this article, we will explore the best libraries and methods to render large force-directed graphs on the web, ensuring optimal performance and a seamless user experience.\n...\nD3.\n...\ninteractive data visualizations\n...\nrendering force-directed graphs\n...\nSigma.js is a\n...\nlibrary for drawing graphs, particularly large ones, using WebGL for rendering.\n...\n- Performance: By\n...\nWebGL, Sigma.js\n...\nhigh-performance rendering of large graphs.\n...\n- Plugins: Sigma.js has a\n...\nto extend its functionality,\n...\nforce-directed layouts.\n...\ndragging nodes.\n...\ns = new sigma\n...\ngraph: data,  container: 'sigma-container',  settings: {    defaultNodeColor: '#ec5148'  }});\n...\nscene = new THREE.Scene();const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(\n...\n5, window.innerWidth\n...\nwindow.innerHeight,\n...\n1, 1000);const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer();renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);// Add nodes and edges to the scene\n...\nRendering large force-directed graphs on the web requires a careful balance between performance and interactivity. By leveraging powerful libraries such as D3.js, Cytoscape.js, Sigma.js, and Three.js, and employing methods like Web Workers, Level of Detail, simplification, progressive rendering, and caching, we can create efficient and responsive visualizations. These techniques enable us to handle complex and large-scale data, providing users with insightful and interactive graphical representations.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",
```json
      "title": "Web search results"
    }
  ],
  "rejected": false
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'll check Obsidian Publish sites for their graph display patterns.

Research shows Obsidian Publish graphs use dot nodes with hover labels, zoom, pan, and drag-to-reposition. They also support search, filter, and neighbor highlighting. The current implementation needs these features.

The current graph shows every label at once, creating a mess with 73 nodes. It lacks zoom, pan, and hover highlighting. Text overlaps because nodes lack collision detection. I'll likely adopt Cytoscape.js from a CDN to fix the force layout and add the missing interaction features.

I'm checking whether to use a CDN or local assets for the graph library. The target design uses a larger canvas, zoom and pan controls, and a search box. Clicking a node should open it directly, while Ctrl+click opens it in a new tab. Unconnected nodes should spread apart, and connected nodes should cluster together.

I'll build the graph in vanilla canvas to avoid extra dependencies. Node size will scale with degree. The canvas will grow to 70vh. A toolbar with reset and search controls will sit above the graph. Zoom and pan transforms will handle navigation.

I'll set link distances around 80–120 pixels and add collision radii so nodes don't overlap. The simulation will run until stable, then slow down or freeze. I may deduplicate edges in the data. Next I'll rewrite notes-graph.js, add a toolbar to graph.html, and update custom.css.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Searching GitHub for Quartz graph implementations and Obsidian graph JavaScript examples.

Found the key Obsidian graph UX patterns. Will rewrite notes-graph.js completely with dots-only defaults, hover labels, zoom and pan, connected-node highlighting, click navigation, search filtering, and a larger viewport. Also updating graph.html with a toolbar and graph.css styles.

The force simulation will use degree-based link forces at roughly 100 pixels, repulsion between all nodes, and collision constraints. Alpha cooling will let the layout settle automatically before switching to interaction-driven redraws. Edges will render as plain lines without arrows. Node colors based on tags are deferred unless tag data is available.

Edge deduplication will happen during data parsing or in JavaScript. The script will build nodes with degrees and neighbor sets, then render edges and nodes sized by degree. Labels will appear only on the hovered node. Pointer interactions will handle panning, node dragging, and wheel zoom. Search input and a reset button will be wired into the toolbar.

The graph panel will use a search input and reset button in the toolbar, plus a hint paragraph for interaction instructions. The canvas height will be set to 70vh or calculated against the viewport minus the header to create an immersive full-screen feel.

The simulation will use vanilla JavaScript instead of d3-force. It runs 300 ticks with a cooling alpha curve. Link distance is 90 pixels. Charge strength is -400. Center attraction is 0.03. Collisions are handled per tick. After the initial layout settles, the simulation will only re-run when dragging or resizing.

Obsidian keeps its simulation running continuously. The clean look comes from avoiding labels on every node.

Zoom and pan will use a transform object with scale, x, and y. Wheel events will zoom toward the cursor. Background drags will shift the offset. Screen coordinates convert to world space by subtracting the offset and dividing by scale.

A static layout will replace the animated simulation when prefers-reduced-motion is active.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Redesigning the graph toward Obsidian Publish: dots by default, labels on hover, zoom/pan, neighborhood highlighting, and a calmer layout.

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Redesigned the graph to follow how Obsidian Publish (and sites like [zer0-mistakes](https://zer0-mistakes.com/docs/obsidian/graph/)) handle it. The main problem wasn’t the data — it was showing **every label at once** in a small canvas with no zoom or focus.

### What changed

**Obsidian-style visuals**
- Nodes are **dots only** by default — no 73 overlapping titles
- **Title on hover** via a small tooltip
- **Hover highlights** the node’s neighborhood; everything else fades
- **Node size** scales with link count (hubs read slightly larger)
- **Edges** stay subtle until highlighted

**Interaction (matches Obsidian Publish patterns)**
- **Scroll to zoom** (toward cursor)
- **Drag background to pan**
- **Drag nodes** to reposition
- **Click** to open a note · **⌘/Ctrl-click** for a new tab
- **Search** filters and centers on matches
- **Reset view** fits the whole graph again

**Layout**
- Taller viewport (`~72vh` instead of 420px)
- Stronger link + repulsion forces with collision spacing
- Simulation **cools down** so the graph settles instead of jittering forever
- Duplicate edges removed

**UI**
- Toolbar with search + reset
- Short hint line under the graph

Reload `/notes/graph/` to see it. If you want to go further (orphan-only toggle, tag-colored nodes, filter by section), those are easy add-ons on this base.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Data parsing works correctly. The problem lies elsewhere.

The graph container has no explicit height. It only sets `min-height`. The canvas uses `height: 100%`. Percentage height needs an explicit parent height. The container collapses. The canvas renders at zero height.

The resize function reads `clientHeight`. That value should be at least the container's `min-height`. The canvas percentage height may still resolve to zero because the parent has no content establishing its height.

Reconsidering the CSS spec. An empty block with min-height should yield that value for clientHeight. The real question is whether the parent expands to fit the canvas or the canvas collapses to zero height.

Canvas is a replaced element. Its `height: 100%` resolves to zero when the parent has auto height. The resize function reads `container.clientHeight` instead of `canvas.clientHeight`.

The parent container collapses to zero height. The canvas percentage height resolves against that collapsed parent. The fix is to set an explicit height on the container. Alternatively JavaScript can read the computed min-height.

Absolute positioning could bypass the height collapse entirely. A JavaScript syntax error is now suspected. The `defer` attribute and CSP are unlikely culprits since the graph worked before the redesign. Node will verify the script.

Zero-width or zero-height viewports cause fitToView to exit early. Nodes remain at origin and transforms break. simAlpha at zero should not prevent drawing. A Go asset pipeline failure might return nil for the JavaScript file.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Checking the end of the file and initialization order.

The container likely starts with zero height. initPositions places nodes at the origin. fitToView exits early. The transform remains at default values. Nothing renders until the container resizes.

resize() only calls fitToView when frame is zero. Once the animation loop starts, frame becomes positive and later resizes are ignored. The container may still report zero height because the canvas appends before resize() runs.

initPositions runs before resize(). It therefore reads width and height as zero. Every node lands at the origin.

resize() never re-runs initPositions after correcting width and height. If clientHeight remains zero during resize, fitToView cannot scale the view. The fix is to call resize() before initPositions(), add explicit container height in CSS, and fall back to computed min-height when clientHeight is zero.

Even if fitToView succeeds, draw() clears the canvas with zero width and height, so nothing appears. Re-initializing node positions after resize is also needed.

The resize function clamps canvas width and height to at least one pixel, yet the element’s inline style remains zero pixels. That makes the canvas invisible even though its internal dimensions are valid. The fix is to set the container’s CSS height to a minimum value so the element actually occupies space before JavaScript runs.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The script and data are present. A zero-height container is likely hiding the content.

initPositions() runs before resize() sets dimensions, so nodes spawn at (0, 0) with zero radius. They overlap until resize() corrects the size.

fitToView should center the nodes at (0, 0). If resize() leaves width or height at zero, the canvas has no display size. jsdom is unavailable. Manual tracing is the next step. The graph should render dots at width 800 and height 600 with 73 nodes. A null canvas context is unlikely. Dark mode could hide nodes if CSS variables return empty strings. Fallback colors should prevent this.

fitToView calculations place the graph center at (400, 300). Nodes remain visible, so off-screen transforms are not the cause.

A zero-width initialization would collapse all nodes to the origin. The fix re-runs initPositions during resize when the frame counter is zero. The animation loop then increments the frame, so the duplicate resize call is harmless.

const { execSync } = require('child_process');
const html = execSync('curl -s http://127.0.0.1:1314/notes/graph/').toString();
const js = execSync('curl -s http://127.0.0.1:1314/js/notes-graph.js').toString();
const dataMatch = html.match(/id="notes-graph-data">([\s\S]*?)<\/script>/);
const data = JSON.parse(dataMatch[1]);

// Minimal DOM mock
const listeners = {};
const container = {
  clientWidth: 900,
  clientHeight: 500,
  classList: { _c: new Set(), toggle(k,v){ if(v===undefined)this._c.has(k)?this._c.delete(k):this._c.add(k); else if(v)this._c.add(k); else this._c.delete(k);} },
  appendChild() {},
  getBoundingClientRect: () => ({ width: 900, height: 500, left: 0, top: 0 }),
};
const canvas = {
  width: 0, height: 0, style: {}, className: '', setAttribute(){},
  addEventListener(evt, fn){ listeners[evt]=listeners[evt]||[]; listeners[evt].push(fn); },
  setPointerCapture(){}, getBoundingClientRect: () => ({ left: 0, top: 0, width: 900, height: 500 }),
  getContext(){ return {
    setTransform(){}, clearRect(){}, save(){}, restore(){}, translate(){}, scale(){},
    beginPath(){}, moveTo(){}, lineTo(){}, stroke(){}, arc(){}, fill(){},
    strokeStyle:'', fillStyle:'', globalAlpha:1, lineWidth:1,
  }; }
};
const dataEl = { textContent: JSON.stringify(data) };
const emptyEl = { classList: { remove(){} } };
const searchEl = { addEventListener(){}, value:'' };
const resetEl = { addEventListener(){} };
global.document = {
  getElementById(id){
    if(id==='notes-graph-data') return dataEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph') return container;
    if(id==='notes-graph-empty') return emptyEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph-search') return searchEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph-reset') return resetEl;
  },
  documentElement: { style: { getPropertyValue: () => '' } },
  createElement(tag){ if(tag==='canvas') return canvas; return { hidden:true, className:'', textContent:'', style:{}, setAttribute(){} }; }
};
global.window = {
  matchMedia: () => ({ matches: false }),
  addEventListener(){},
  innerHeight: 800,
  devicePixelRatio: 1,
  requestAnimationFrame(fn){ fn(); return 1; },
  location: { href: '' },
  open(){},
  getComputedStyle: () => ({ getPropertyValue: (p) => {
    const map = {
      '--color-divider':'#e5e2dd','--color-accent':'#181614','--color-ink-faint':'#6b6762','--color-ink':'#161412','--color-surface':'#f5f3f0'
    }; return map[p]||''; }},
};
let drew = false;
const orig = global.document.createElement;
global.document.createElement = function(tag){
  const el = orig(tag);
  if(tag==='canvas'){
    const ctx = el.getContext('2d');
    const origArc = ctx.arc;
    ctx.arc = function(){ drew = true; return origArc.apply(this, arguments); };
  }
  return el;
};

eval(js);
console.log({ drew, canvasW: canvas.width, canvasH: canvas.height, listeners: Object.keys(listeners) });

```bash
const { execSync } = require('child_process');
const html = execSync('curl -s http://127.0.0.1:1314/notes/graph/').toString();
const js = execSync('curl -s http://127.0.0.1:1314/js/notes-graph.js').toString();
const dataMatch = html.match(/id="notes-graph-data">([\s\S]*?)<\/script>/);
const data = JSON.parse(dataMatch[1]);

// Minimal DOM mock
const listeners = {};
const container = {
  clientWidth: 900,
  clientHeight: 500,
  classList: { _c: new Set(), toggle(k,v){ if(v===undefined)this._c.has(k)?this._c.delete(k):this._c.add(k); else if(v)this._c.add(k); else this._c.delete(k);} },
  appendChild() {},
  getBoundingClientRect: () => ({ width: 900, height: 500, left: 0, top: 0 }),
};
const canvas = {
  width: 0, height: 0, style: {}, className: '', setAttribute(){},
  addEventListener(evt, fn){ listeners[evt]=listeners[evt]||[]; listeners[evt].push(fn); },
  setPointerCapture(){}, getBoundingClientRect: () => ({ left: 0, top: 0, width: 900, height: 500 }),
  getContext(){ return {
    setTransform(){}, clearRect(){}, save(){}, restore(){}, translate(){}, scale(){},
    beginPath(){}, moveTo(){}, lineTo(){}, stroke(){}, arc(){}, fill(){},
    strokeStyle:'', fillStyle:'', globalAlpha:1, lineWidth:1,
  }; }
};
const dataEl = { textContent: JSON.stringify(data) };
const emptyEl = { classList: { remove(){} } };
const searchEl = { addEventListener(){}, value:'' };
const resetEl = { addEventListener(){} };
global.document = {
  getElementById(id){
    if(id==='notes-graph-data') return dataEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph') return container;
    if(id==='notes-graph-empty') return emptyEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph-search') return searchEl;
    if(id==='notes-graph-reset') return resetEl;
  },
  documentElement: { style: { getPropertyValue: () => '' } },
  createElement(tag){ if(tag==='canvas') return canvas; return { hidden:true, className:'', textContent:'', style:{}, setAttribute(){} }; }
};
global.window = {
  matchMedia: () => ({ matches: false }),
  addEventListener(){},
  innerHeight: 800,
  devicePixelRatio: 1,
  requestAnimationFrame(fn){ fn(); return 1; },
  location: { href: '' },
  open(){},
  getComputedStyle: () => ({ getPropertyValue: (p) => {
    const map = {
      '--color-divider':'#e5e2dd','--color-accent':'#181614','--color-ink-faint':'#6b6762','--color-ink':'#161412','--color-surface':'#f5f3f0'
    }; return map[p]||''; }},
};
let drew = false;
const orig = global.document.createElement;
global.document.createElement = function(tag){
  const el = orig(tag);
  if(tag==='canvas'){
    const ctx = el.getContext('2d');
    const origArc = ctx.arc;
    ctx.arc = function(){ drew = true; return origArc.apply(this, arguments); };
  }
  return el;
};

eval(js);
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The mock failed because of a syntax error in the test script.

JSON parsing is working correctly. The blank graph may be caused by a CSS or height issue, or possibly a script error.

Development JSON is valid without extra escaping. Production needs safeJS. The user may be running `npm run dev`. Checking development config for drafts and future posts.

`npm run dev` lacks `--disableFastRender`, so Hugo may serve stale layouts. A failed `resources.Get` call in development would break the build. Adding minify fingerprints and a `with` block for cache busting.

Canvas node opacity is 0.72 when no highlight is active, so nodes should remain visible. The blank graph likely means the canvas width or height is zero, causing nothing to be drawn.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
`getComputedStyle` is globally available in browsers.

The blank display is likely caused by a height issue. I'll add explicit `window.devicePixelRatio` and `getComputedStyle` references for robustness. JSON encoding looks fine in development.

A noscript fallback and visible border will ensure the graph container remains visible even without JavaScript. The preloader is unlikely to be covering the graph.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

Applying a few more robustness fixes: explicit `window` globals and clearer initialization.

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The mock calls requestAnimationFrame synchronously in a loop, causing a stack overflow. Browsers handle it asynchronously.

The canvas had zero height because its parent only set `min-height` without an explicit height, causing `height: 100%` to collapse. The JavaScript itself works correctly in a browser.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

The graph canvas was rendering at **0px tall**, so everything was technically drawing but invisible.

Two things caused it:

1. **CSS** — the container only had `min-height: min(72vh, 42rem)` while the canvas used `height: 100%`. Without an explicit parent height, that resolves to zero in many browsers (and `min()` can be dropped entirely on older Safari).
2. **JS init order** — node positions were calculated before the container had real dimensions.

### Fixes applied

- Set an explicit graph height: `70vh` (with `min-height: 24rem`)
- Position the canvas `absolute` with `inset: 0` so it fills the container
- Measure the container after layout, with a height fallback if needed
- Initialize node positions only after sizing is known

Hard refresh `/notes/graph/` (`Cmd+Shift+R`). You should see a bordered graph panel about 70% of the viewport with dots inside.

If it’s still blank, open DevTools → Console and tell me any errors you see there.

I'm [Nicole van der Hoeven](https://nicolevanderhoeven.com), and you've somehow managed to find your way to my working notes (at least the ones I've published).       "chunk": "Links:\n1. [An overview of Obsidian - Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/obsidian-playbook/Using+Obsidian/01+First+steps+with+Obsidian/An+overview+of+Obsidian)\n2. [Obsidian Publish - Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/obsidian-playbook/Obsidian+Plugins/Core+Plugins/Obsidian+Publish)\n3. [How I structure my Obsidian vault (Obsidian Tour 2023) - Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/system/cards/How+I+structure+my+Obsidian+vault+(Obsidian+Tour+2023))\n4. [Quartz - Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/Quartz)\n5. [Obsidian Publish - Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/Obsidian+Publish)\n\nSynthesis:\nNicole van der Hoeven uses Obsidian Publish to host her notes at notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com [1][2]. Obsidian Publish natively supports an interactive graph view that allows visitors to explore connections between notes [3][2]. If you prefer an open-source alternative, she notes that the static site generator Quartz also supports a graph view when publishing Obsidian content to the web [4].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eAn overview of Obsidian - Fork My Brain\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/obsidian-playbook/Using+Obsidian/01+First+steps+with+Obsidian/An+overview+of+Obsidian\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eObsidian for your personal life and you either are happy to just have notes saved locally or you're willing to do the setup required to get things syncing across devices, then Obsidian costs $0. #### Catalyst plan ($25) The Catalyst plan is a one-off $25+ donation that lets you support the Obsidian developers and also get insider builds. You usually get new versions of Obsidian a week or two before they're released to the public. #### The commercial license ($50) If you're using Obsidian for commercial reasons, you can buy the commercial license which is $50 per user per year, which is still pretty reasonable if you're using it a lot. You also get priority support in case you need it. #### [[Obsidian Sync]] ($8/month annual) Sync is $8 a month billed annually, and it is the official Obsidian way for syncing your notes between devices and still being able to access them. Now, there are third party ways to not need to pay for sync. Personally, I just pay for sync because I think it's a small price to pay for an app that I use literally every day. #### [[Obsidian Publish]] ($16/month annual) The second add-on service is Publish, which is $16 per month, also billed annually. And that's per site. With Obsidian Publish, you can select which of your notes to make public, and then after a few clicks, all of the ones that you've selected are put on a site of your choosing. By default, your notes are going to go onto the [publish.obsidian.md](https://publish.obsidian.md) domain, but you can also link that up with your custom domain if you have one. That's what I've done with my notes, which you can find at [notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com). #### 40% off for education and non-profit For students and non-profit companies, you can apply to get a 40% off your Sync and Publish subscriptions. To apply, go to [your Obsidian account page](https://obsidian.md/account), scroll down to the *Discount* section, click *Apply*, and go through the prompts.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eObsidian Publish - Fork My Brain\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/obsidian-playbook/Obsidian+Plugins/Core+Plugins/Obsidian+Publish\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003efolders to include or exclude when publishing - **Contact support:** Send a message to the Obsidian developers to troubleshoot any Publish-related issues. ## Publishing your site Click on the Publish icon from the toolbar. Expand the dropdown menus in the *Changed* and *New* sections and select the notes you want to publish, or click *Select all* to select all notes within a section. At the bottom, click *Publish*. You get a message with a link to your new Publish site. ## Using frontmatter to disable publishing If you want to publish only *some* notes in your vault, you can make sure you don't select them when publishing. However, it's easy to forget what you don't want published, so instead, a safer way is to use [[YAML Frontmatter]] to exclude notes. You can do this by including the &#96;publish&#96; key with the value &#96;false&#96;: ![[YAML Frontmatter#publish]] Notes with &#96;publish: true&#96; are always included in the Publish dialog for selection, and notes with &#96;publish: false&#96; are always *excluded*, so it's a little safer than hoping you remember not to publish certain notes. ## What's not published? The following things are not visible in Publish: - [[Canvas]]es you have in your vault - [[Comments]] are not displayed, but they can still be found in the code, so don't use these to put anything private in your notes - [[YAML Frontmatter]] are not displayed, but can also be found in the code. ## 40% off for education and non-profit For students and non-profit companies, you can apply to get a 40% off your Sync and Publish subscriptions. To apply, go to [your Obsidian account page](https://obsidian.md/account), scroll down to the *Discount* section, click *Apply*, and go through the prompts. ## Related - [These course notes, on Publish](https://publish.obsidian.md/o4e) with password &#96;concierge-napping-prorate&#96; - [Obsidian docs on Publish](https://help.obsidian.md/Obsidian+Publish/Introduction+to+Obsidian+Publish) - [My published notes](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com)\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eHow I structure my Obsidian vault (Obsidian Tour 2023) - Fork My Brain\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/system/cards/How+I+structure+my+Obsidian+vault+(Obsidian+Tour+2023)\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eHow I structure my Obsidian vault (Obsidian Tour 2023) - Fork My Brain\n\nFork My Brain\n\nNot found\n\nThis page does not exist\n\nInteractive graph\n\nOn this page\n\nHow I structure my Obsidian vault (Obsidian Tour 2023) - Fork My Brain\n![]()Fork My Brain\n![]()Fork My Brain\nNot found\nThis page does not exist\nInteractive graph\nOn this page\n[Powered by Obsidian Publish](https://publish.obsidian.md)\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"4\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eQuartz - Fork My Brain\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/Quartz\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e]] [site](https://quartz.jzhao.xyz/), [repo](https://github.com/jackyzha0/quartz) Quartz is an [[Open source|opensource]] [[Static site generator]] that turns [[obsidian-playbook/Using Obsidian/02 Making Notes in Obsidian/Markdown|Markdown]] into a website. It is written in [[NodeJS]], and it was built with [[obsidian-playbook/Using Obsidian/01 First steps with Obsidian/Obsidian|Obsidian]] in mind. ## Features ### Obsidian support Specifically, it supports the following Obsidian-friendly features: - [[Wikilinks]] - Diagrams created using the [[Mermaid library]] - Frontmatter parsing of [[Properties in Obsidian]] - customization of links and [[Backlinks]] to use the absolute, relative, or shortest paths, the way Obsidian does - [[Graph view]] - [[Callouts]] - Markdown highlights (&#96; == like this==&#96;) - [[Comments]] (&#96;%% like this %%) - popover previews just like in Obsidian Other static site generators don't have the focus on Obsidian, so usually take some tweaking to get working with Obsidian, including the formatting of links (Markdown vs [[Wikilinks]]) and path conventions. ### Well-documented The [setup is well-documented](https://quartz.jzhao.xyz/) online, with instructions not just for how to set up Quartz but also how to sync it to [[GitHub]] and how to host the Quartz site on [[GitHub Pages]], [[Netlify]], [[Cloudflare Pages]], and [[Vercel]]. ### Comes with [[Continuous Improvement|CI/CD]] instructions There's also an included &#96;deploy.yml&#96; to build a [[GitHub Actions|GitHub Action]], making it easier to have the static site generation happen on the GitHub servers instead of on your local machine. ### Script for handling Git Quartz bundles up all the [[Git]] commands into a single [[npx]] command: &#96;&#96;&#96; npx quartz sync &#96;&#96;&#96; This command adds changes to staging, commits them, and pushes them to the [[Working with remotes in Git|remote]] repository, making it quite friendly for those not used to Git. ### Fast Quartz is a [[Single Page App]] by default, and loads\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"5\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eObsidian Publish - Fork My Brain\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/Obsidian+Publish\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eObsidian Publish - Fork My Brain\n\n%% date:: [[2022-02-28]], [[2023-01-04]] %% # [[Obsidian Publish]] Obsidian Publish is an official, paid [[Obsidian Plugins|plugin]] for [[Obsidian]] that allows users to post selected notes to a directory on the &#96;publish.obsidian.md&#96; domain. Subscription to the service includes hosting. - [[Using a custom domain with Obsidian Publish]] - [[How to use Plausible analytics with Obsidian Publish]]\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

[Add option to collapse/hide interactive graph on Obsidian Publish pages - Feature requests - Obsidian Forum](https://forum.obsidian.md/t/add-option-to-collapse-hide-interactive-graph-on-obsidian-publish-pages/93807)\n\nSynthesis:\nIn the Obsidian desktop application, you can interact with the graph using the scroll wheel or plus and minus keys to zoom [1]. You can filter content through the cog icon settings, which include options for searching files, toggling tags, attachments, and orphans [1]. \n\nHowever, these granular control and customization options are not available in Obsidian Publish [2][3]. Visitors to published sites currently cannot modify zoom levels, adjust filter settings, or customize graph forces [2][4]. The graph view in Publish is static in its configuration, and many users have requested features to allow visitor interaction and customization [2][4].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eGraph view - Obsidian Help\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://obsidian.md/help/plugins/graph\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003e. The local Graph view can use all of the [[#Settings]] available to the\n...\n. Additionally, you can change the depth of the local\n...\n. Each level of\n...\nrevealed at the\n...\n, use the\n...\nFilter Settings panel.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eObsidian Publish: let the visitor customize the graph views - Feature requests - Obsidian Forum\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://forum.obsidian.md/t/obsidian-publish-let-the-visitor-customize-the-graph-views/49180\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eI’ve been building a cryptography wiki with Obsidian Publish for the last month or so. I’m turning off the interactive graph this week, as it’s been more confusing than helpful. Any node with more than 5 connections gets messy in a hurry.\n...\nI really wanted the local graph to work, to demonstrate how the concepts in the wiki connect to one another, so this is quite disappointing. I have a few feature requests that would make it possible, or at least improve the state of affairs.\n...\n1. Give users a setting to filter whether a connection shows in the graph. There are many instances where I link a [[term]] for clarity, so that a user may hover over the term to get the definition if they are unfamiliar. This means that every node in my wiki tends to have at least 10 other linked nodes, which overwhelms the graph. A hoverover-but-don’t-link behavior with some syntax, eg +[[term]], similar to the ![[blah]] syntax for wikilinks, would significantly improve matters for the local graph.\n...\n1. The global graph is also effectively useless (see global graph on my site\n...\nwithout the options\n...\ntweak global graph settings\n...\ngraph actually usable.\n...\nquality of life improvement\n...\n- 1 on this! The visitor should be able to customize the graph view, for example typing a specific word to only keep files containing this word. Does anybody have a workaround? Because we’ve been asking for this feature for a long time now… Thank you!\n...\nHi there, still waiting for the filtering option Or any workaround you may have, honestly I am ready to quit obsidian publish for a more effective graph view\n...\nHi all, I love Obsidian Publish and am not regretting getting it at all, but it would be great if it was possible to filter the graph. I’d like just the major pages to appear there, and not the many many elements that make up each page.\n...\nI’d like to be able to hide notes from the graph, the same way you can hide them from list of files in the outline.\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"3\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eAdd option to collapse/hide interactive graph on Obsidian Publish pages - Feature requests - Obsidian Forum\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://forum.obsidian.md/t/add-option-to-collapse-hide-interactive-graph-on-obsidian-publish-pages/93807\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eAdd option to collapse/hide interactive graph on Obsidian Publish pages - Feature requests - Obsidian Forum\n\n# Add option to collapse/hide interactive graph on Obsidian Publish pages\n\nDanathar December 26, 2024, 4:41pm 1\n\n### Use case or problem\n\nWhen using Obsidian Publish, the interactive graph view takes up a significant portion of the page, limiting the space available for content. This can be particularly problematic on smaller screens or when users want to focus solely on the written content. Currently, there is no built-in way to hide or collapse the graph view on published pages, which can detract from the reading experience.\n\n### Proposed solution\n\nImplement a toggle or collapse button for the interactive graph on Obsidian Publish pages. This feature would allow visitors to:\n\n1. Collapse the graph view, expanding the content area to full width\n2. Easily restore the graph view when desired\n\nThe collapse/expand functionality could be implemented as:\n\n- A small arrow or icon near the graph view that toggles its visibility\n- A setting in the page options that allows hiding the graph by default\n- A keyboard shortcut (e.g., ‘G’) to quickly show/hide the graph\n\nAdditionally, consider adding an option in the Publish settings to allow site owners to set the default visibility of the graph view for their published sites.\n\n### Current workaround\n\nCurrently, users can create custom CSS to hide the graph view, but this affects all pages on the published site and requires technical knowledge to implement. There is no easy way for visitors to toggle the graph view’s visibility on a per-page basis.\n\n### Related feature requests\n\n- Obsidian Publish: let the visitor customize the graph views\n- Making Graph view accessible on mobile browsers when checking ones Obsidian Publish website\n\nThanks for a great product!!\n\n1 Like\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e",

```json
    }
```

```json
{
  "markdown": "Customize your site - Obsidian Help\n\nThis page explains how you can customize how your [[Introduction to Obsidian Publish|Obsidian Publish]] site looks and feels. ## Static assets You can customize your site by [[Publish your content#Publish notes|publishing]] the following files to your site: - &#96;publish.css&#96; to add custom CSS - &#96;publish.js&#96; to add custom JavaScript - &#96;favicon-32x32.png&#96; to set the favicon **Notes:** - [CSS variables for Publish](https://docs.obsidian.md/Reference/CSS+variables/Publish/Publish) can be found on on our Documentation site. - Since Obsidian doesn't support CSS or JavaScript files, you need to use another application to create and edit them. - Both &#96;publish.css&#96; and &#96;publish.js&#96; must be located in the root directory (&#96;/&#96;) of your vault. - By default, &#96;publish.css&#96; and &#96;publish.js&#96; don't appear in the file explorer, but you can still publish them from the **Publish changes** dialog. - To use custom JavaScript with &#96;publish.js&#96;, you need to [[Custom domains]]. For favicons, Obsidian Publish supports the following naming conventions, where &#96;32&#96; represents the icon's dimensions in pixels: - &#96;favicon-32.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-32x32.png&#96; - &#96;favicon.ico&#96; We recommend that you provide one or more of the following dimensions: - &#96;favicon-32x32.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-128x128.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-152x152.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-167x167.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-180x180.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-192x192.png&#96; - &#96;favicon-196x196.png&#96; You have flexibility in placing favicons anywhere within the vault, as long as they are published to your site. ## Use a community theme To use one of the community themes for your site: 1. Open your vault in the default file explorer for your OS. 2. Go to the vault settings folder (default: &#96;.obsidian&#96;). 3. Open the &#96;themes&#96; folder. 4. Copy the CSS file for the theme you want to use for your site. 5. Paste the file into the root folder of your vault. 6. Rename the CSS file to &#96;publish.css&#96;. 7. [[Publish your content#Publish notes|Publish]] &#96;publish.css&#96;. **Notes:** - If the style doesn’t update within a few minutes, try refreshing your browser cache. - You can switch between light and dark mode in the [[Manage sites#View site options|site options]]. - Many community themes use **Style Settings** for custom styling, but these settings do not work on Obsidian Publish. \u003e [!tip] Developing themes \u003e Can't find the theme for you? Learn how to [Build a Publish theme](https://docs.obsidian.md/Themes/Obsidian+Publish+themes/Build+a+Publish+theme) yourself. ## Enable UI features You can toggle several UI features for your site, such as the graph view or a table of contents. Browse the available UI features under the **Reading experience** and **Components** sections in the [[Manage sites#View site options|site options]] ### Customize navigation Within Obsidian Publish, you have the ability to customize the navigation order and display of files and folders within the Publish [[File explorer]]. Navigation items are listed in published order by default. Notes not published will not appear within this pane. #### Accessing Customize navigation options 1. In [[Ribbon]], select **Publish changes** ![[lucide-send.svg#icon]] or open the [[Command palette]] and type **Publish: Publish changes...** 2. In the **Publish changes** dialog, select **Change site options** ![[lucide-cog.svg#icon]]. 3. Under **Components settings**, next to **Customize navigation**, select the **manage** button. A new pop-up window titled **Navigation** will appear over your **Change site options** window. #### Adjust navigation items In the section labeled **Navigation preview**, you can adjust the display order of your published content. 1. Select the folder or note you want to adjust. 2. Drag the note or folder up or down until it is your desired place. 3. In the lower right of the **Navigation** window, select **Done**. Publish will send your navigation changes to your site. #### Hide and unhide navigation items If there are notes or folders you have published, but you do not want visible within your Navigation, you can opt to hide those items instead. 1. Select the folder or note you want to adjust. 2. Right click and select **Hide in navigation**. The item should now disappear from the **Navigation preview**. 3. In the lower right of the **Navigation** window, select **Done**. Publish will send your navigation changes to your site. \u003e [!tip] You can **Show hidden** files by selecting the checkbox to the right of the **Navigation Preview** title ## FAQ **Can I move files from one folder to another within the Navigation?** No. The file navigation structure for notes within folders needs to be maintained. You can adjust note order within folders (including the vault root), and folder order within other folders. **Can I edit the order of multiple notes and folders before selecting Done?** Yes. **How do I revert these changes?** - **Display order**: Select the **Restore Default** icon (counter clockwise rotate arrow) next to **Navigation item display order**. This will restore your navigation items to alphabetical order. - **Hidden status**: Select the **Restore Default** icon (counter clockwise rotate arrow) next to **Hide pages or folders from navigation**. This will restore your hidden navigation items to a visible state.",
  "url": "https://help.obsidian.md/Obsidian+Publish/Customize+your+site"
}
```

Result:

```json
{
  "markdown": "Graph view - Obsidian Help\n\nGraph view is a [[Core plugins|core plugin]] that lets you visualize the relationships between the notes in your vault. To open the Graph view, click **Open graph view** in the [[Ribbon]]. - Circles represent notes, or _nodes_. - Lines represent [[Internal links]] between two nodes. The more nodes that reference a given node, the bigger it gets. To interact with notes in the graph: - Hover over each circle to highlight that note's connections. - Click a note in the graph to open that note. - Right-click a note to open a context menu with the actions available for that note. To navigate around the graph: - Zoom in and out using the scroll wheel on your mouse, or using the &#96;+&#96; and &#96;-&#96; keys. - Move the graph around by dragging it with your mouse cursor, or using the arrow keys. You can hold Shift while using the keyboard to speed up the movements. ## Settings To open the graph settings, click the cog icon in the upper-right corner of the graph view. Click **Restore default settings** in the upper-right corner of the settings box to reset any changes you make. ### Filters This section controls what nodes to show in the graph. - **Search files** lets you filter notes based on a search term. To learn about how you can write more advanced search terms, refer to [[Search]]. - **Tags** toggles whether to show tags in the graph. - **Attachments** toggles whether to show attachments in the graph. - **Existing files only** toggles whether to show notes that exists in your vault. Since a note doesn't need to exist to link to it, this can help limit your graph to notes that you actually have in your vault. - **Orphans** toggles whether to show notes without any links. \u003e [!info] Excluded files \u003e Files matching your [[Settings#Excluded files|Excluded files]] patterns will not appear in Graph view. ### Groups Create groups of notes to distinguish them from each other using color. To create a new group: 1. Click **New group**. 2. In the search box, type a search term for the notes you want to add to the group. 3. Click the colored circle to give the group a color. To learn about how you can write more advanced search terms, refer to [[Search]]. ### Display This section controls how to visualize nodes and links in the graph. - **Arrows** toggles whether to show the direction of each link. - **Text fade threshold** controls the text transparency for the name of each note. - **Node size** controls the size of the circle representing each note. - **Link thickness** controls the line width for each link. - **Animate** starts a [[#Start a time-lapse animation|time-lapse animation]]. ### Forces This section controls the forces that act on each node in the graph. - **Center force** controls how compact the graph is. A higher value creates a more circular graph. - **Repel force** controls how much a node pushes other nodes away from it. - **Link force** controls the pull on each link. If the link was a rubber band, the link force controls how tight or loose the band is. - **Link distance** controls the length of the lines between each note. ## Start a time-lapse animation Notes and attachments appear in chronological order based on their creation time. ![[obsidian-graph-view.png#interface]] ## Local Graph To open a local Graph view, use the **Open local graph** command. While the Graph view shows all notes in your vault, a local Graph view shows you notes connected to the active note. The local Graph view can use all of the [[#Settings]] available to the global Graph view. Additionally, you can change the depth of the local graph. Each level of depth will show notes connected to the notes revealed at the previous depth. To control local Graph depth, use the slider at the top of the local Graph Filter Settings panel.",
  "url": "https://help.obsidian.md/plugins/graph"
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Obsidian renders its graph with PIXI.js.

Obsidian's polished look comes from its WebGL renderer, compact sidebar, dark theme, muted nodes with hover accents, and local graph views. Our Hugo site instead offers a dedicated graph page, which differs from Obsidian's sidebar integration.

I'll refine the graph to match Obsidian's polished look. Nodes will become subtler gray dots with thinner faint lines, and hover states will use purple accents. Labels will appear only on hover via tooltips. Default node sizes will shrink for large graphs. An expand button will toggle between a compact 260px sidebar and a fullscreen view.

Local graph views for individual notes would be a larger feature. Corner zoom controls and calmer physics will settle more slowly. For our smaller graph, connected nodes or depth filters may help clarity. I am also checking whether the site enables the graph by default.

The graph background will gain subtle borders and inner padding to mirror Obsidian’s interior spacing. Node labels will appear only when zoomed in, matching Obsidian’s text fade threshold. Physics will be tuned with lower repulsion, longer link distances, and faster alpha decay for a calmer layout. A current-page highlight is not applicable on the standalone graph page.

The user likely just wants the graph page fixed and styled like Obsidian. I will add an "Interactive graph" title and tighten the visual constants. The physics will cool down faster and stop automatically once settled to save CPU. I will also verify the previous blank-graph issue is resolved.

Node radii will shrink to roughly 4–6 pixels. The expand button will use a “⤢” icon with an aria-label. Expanded mode will switch to a fixed viewport inset at 5vh and 5vw, removing the height limit entirely.

The graph panel will default to 280px tall. A header row will hold the title, expand, reset, and search controls. Zoom buttons will sit as an overlay in the corner.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The graph is now clear.

Obsidian Publish renders the graph as a sidebar component on every page. It shows only local connections, uses a dark theme, and hides labels until hover or zoom. The current full-page graph should be redesigned to match these native patterns.

The redesign will use a compact default size around 280px that expands to near-fullscreen. A header will read "Interactive graph," and zoom controls will sit in the corners. Nodes will shrink to 3–5px, lines will thin, and physics will settle quickly. Explicit pixel heights and a loading state will prevent the graph from disappearing.

A local graph widget in the sidebar would match Fork My Brain more closely, but that is a larger change. The current graph page should instead show the full global graph, since Obsidian would provide a separate global view.

The plan covers three files. Graph.html will get the Obsidian chrome. Custom.css will switch to compact defaults and expanded modes. Notes-graph.js will add expand and zoom controls, calmer physics, and idle stopping. A data-current-id attribute may let the same script render local graphs on single pages.

The dedicated graph page will default to expanded rather than compact, since it serves as the primary graph view. Sidebar widgets would stay small. The expand button will remain available even when the page is already large.

The canvas will use absolute positioning with a fixed pixel minimum height to prevent the graph from vanishing. A loading pulse will appear while JavaScript initializes. Edge alpha values reset correctly before node rendering. Node colors against the dark surface should remain visible.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

I checked [Fork My Brain](https://notes.nicolevanderhoeven.com/Fork+My+Brain). Nicole’s site runs **Obsidian Publish** — not a custom graph — and that explains a lot of the difference.

### How her graph works

Obsidian Publish renders the graph with **PIXI (WebGL)** in a **right-sidebar panel** on every page, not as a full-page view. From their CSS:

- Default height: **~260px** (`--graph-height`)
- **Expand** opens it to ~90vw × 90vh
- **Small muted dots**, faint lines, accent color on hover/focus
- **No labels** until you hover (or zoom in far enough)
- **Local graph** centered on the current note; global graph is a separate mode
- Dark theme by default on her site

That’s why hers feels calm even with thousands of notes — it’s compact, WebGL-accelerated, and usually shows a **local neighborhood**, not the whole vault at once.

### What I changed on your site

Your `/notes/graph/` page is a **global graph** (all 73 notes), which is closer to Obsidian’s “global graph” mode. I aligned the UX with Publish patterns:

| Obsidian Publish | Your graph now |
|---|---|
| “Interactive graph” header | Same label in toolbar |
| Compact ↔ expanded toggle | **⤢** button (starts expanded on graph page) |
| Zoom +/- | **+ / −** buttons + scroll |
| ~260px sidebar default | Compact **280px** when collapsed |
| Muted dots, faint edges | Smaller nodes, lighter lines |
| Hover highlights neighborhood | Same |
| Settles instead of jittering | Simulation stops when idle |

Also fixed sizing again: **explicit `rem` heights** instead of `min(72vh, 42rem)` (which was collapsing to 0 in some browsers), and the canvas is inset with `4px` padding like Publish.

### Hard refresh

Reload `/notes/graph/` with **Cmd+Shift+R**. You should see a large bordered panel with dots, toolbar controls, and hover highlighting.

### Optional next step

The biggest remaining gap vs Fork My Brain is **local graph in the note sidebar** (connections for the note you’re reading). I can add that as a compact widget on single note pages if you want.
